items.mistake: Trusting a notebook that was run out of order.
items.fix: Finish with Restart & Run All before sharing or concluding.
items.why: Hidden state can make broken notebooks appear to work.
items.example: Cell 5 references a variable defined in a now-deleted cell 12. The notebook 'works' for you (kernel still has the variable), but a clean Restart & Run All immediately raises `NameError` — that's the real state of the code.

items.mistake: Re-running expensive setup (model load, API client) in every cell.
items.fix: Initialize once in an early cell and rely on the persistent kernel.
items.why: Wastes time and money; the kernel already holds the objects.
items.example:
```python
# Cell 2 — once per session
model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')   # 200 MB, 5s
# Cells 3..50 — instant reuse
emb = model.encode(text)
```

items.mistake: Assuming In [n] reflects a cell's position on the page.
items.fix: Read In [n] as execution order; positions and order can differ.
items.why: Misreading it hides out-of-order execution problems.
items.example: If cell at position 3 shows `In [9]` and cell at position 4 shows `In [4]`, position-4 ran *before* position-3's most recent run — any dependency from 3 to 4 may be stale.

items.mistake: Leaving an infinite/long cell running and re-running others.
items.fix: Interrupt the kernel to stop just that cell.
items.why: A busy kernel queues further executions, freezing the notebook.
items.example: Cell spinning on `for url in big_list: requests.get(url)`? Hit Kernel → Interrupt (Esc + I + I). The cell stops, the kernel stays alive, and queued cells start running again.
